claimText: 3D printing is an additive manufacturing process that creates three-dimensional objects by laying down successive layers of material.
evidenceExcerpt: 3D printing is an additive process creating objects by laying down successive layers of material.
examUse: Statement-based question distinguishing additive manufacturing from subtractive manufacturing.

claimText: 3D printing has diverse applications including bioprinting organs, reconstructing fossils, and forensic pathology.
evidenceExcerpt: Applications of 3D printing include bioprinting organs (organ on chip), reconstructing fossils, and forensic pathology.
examUse: Application-based question identifying valid use cases of 3D printing.
distractorHooks: Restricted solely to industrial plastic manufacturing

claimText: 4D printing differs from 3D printing by incorporating the dimension of time, allowing objects to transform after being printed.
evidenceExcerpt: 4D printing evolved from 3D printing by adding the dimension of time.
examUse: Comparison question highlighting the difference between 3D and 4D printing.
distractorHooks: Printing using four different materials simultaneously

claimText: Objects created using 4D printing can change their shape or function in response to environmental stimuli like heat, light, or moisture.
evidenceExcerpt: 4D printed objects can change shape or function over time in response to environmental stimuli such as heat, light, or moisture.
examUse: Statement-based question on the mechanism of transformation in 4D printed objects.
distractorHooks: Changes are driven by internal battery-powered motors
